title: CBRT Raises Inflation Forecast to 26%, NATO Pipeline Bid, and Flotilla Departs Marmaris
subhead: Turkey's central bank revised its year-end inflation forecast sharply upward on Iran war fallout, Ankara proposed a $1.2B NATO fuel pipeline to Romania, and 54 vessels sailed from Marmaris toward Gaza — while the PKK peace process stalled and Ankara deepened ties with Kazakhstan and Iraq.

sections.heading: CBRT Raises 2026 Inflation Forecast to 26% as Energy Costs Surge
sections.body:
Governor Fatih Karahan announced the Central Bank of the Republic of Türkiye raised its year-end inflation forecast from 18% to 26%, abandoning its previous range-based guidance due to heightened uncertainty from the Iran war. Annual inflation hit 32.4% in April, up from 30.9% in March, with energy inflation surging 19 percentage points to 47% over two months alone. Electricity and natural gas tariffs were revised upward following higher import costs.

The bank cut its policy rate by 100 basis points to 37% in January but has held steady since, suspending one-week repo auctions in March to push the reference rate to around 40%. FX reserves rose from $41.6 billion to $53.2 billion, backed by $110 billion in gold — reflecting hot-money inflows from the carry trade. The lira has depreciated only 5% this year, trading at 45.17 per dollar. The IMF cut its 2026 growth forecast for Turkey to 3.4%, and the manufacturing PMI fell to 46 — its lowest since September 2024.
sections.whyItMatters: The Iran war has blown apart Turkey's disinflation trajectory. The CBRT's 8-percentage-point forecast revision in one quarter signals that the war's energy shock is eroding the hard-won gains from two years of tight policy — and the bank is now explicitly trading growth for currency stability.

sections.heading: Turkey Proposes $1.2B NATO Fuel Pipeline to Romania
sections.body:
Ahead of the July 7–8 NATO Summit in Ankara, Turkey has proposed building a $1.2 billion fuel pipeline to supply NATO's eastern European flank through Romania. According to Bloomberg, the pipeline would transport military-grade fuel westward, bolstering alliance logistics in a region dependent on vulnerable maritime supply lines.

The proposal positions Turkey as a critical energy and logistics hub for NATO at a moment when the alliance's eastern members face heightened threat perceptions. It also dovetails with Ankara's broader effort to showcase strategic relevance ahead of hosting the summit — the first extraordinary NATO summit in Turkey's history.
sections.whyItMatters: The pipeline bid reframes Turkey's role from political problem-child within NATO to indispensable logistics node — and gives Ankara a concrete deliverable to announce at the July summit.

sections.heading: Global Sumud Flotilla Departs Marmaris with 54 Vessels
sections.body:
54 vessels carrying over 500 activists departed Marmaris on May 14, the eve of Nakba Day, in the Global Sumud Flotilla's second attempt to break Israel's Gaza blockade. The first attempt saw 20 vessels intercepted by the Israeli Navy near Crete on April 29, with 173 activists deposited on Greek shores. Steering committee member Saif Abu Keshek — deported from Israel last Sunday — announced the departure at a press conference on May 13.

Greenpeace's Arctic Sunrise, which was part of the original convoy, appeared to remain near Crete and did not rejoin. Israel's Foreign Ministry called the flotilla a propaganda stunt, alleging the ships carried no humanitarian aid. The flotilla's declared mission is to raise awareness of the Gaza blockade rather than deliver large-scale aid.
sections.whyItMatters: Departing from Turkish territory for the second time puts Ankara in an awkward diplomatic position — the flotilla is a civilian initiative, not a government operation, but its repeated use of Turkish ports inevitably draws Israel's attention to Turkey's role as a launching point.

sections.heading: PKK Peace Process Stalls as Disarmament Dispute Deepens
sections.body:
Reuters reports that Turkey's peace process with the PKK has stalled over how to implement agreements, with both sides waiting for the other to act first. Ankara insists on complete disarmament before any legal reforms; the PKK demands legal and political guarantees before laying down arms, citing instability from the Iran war. Abdullah Öcalan's February 2025 call for dissolution and the PKK's subsequent ceasefire remain the process's only breakthroughs.

In early May, MHP leader Devlet Bahçeli proposed a 'Peace and Politicization Coordination Office' to allow Öcalan a role in monitoring implementation — but the government has not formally endorsed it. The parliamentary commission monitoring the process approved a report in February 2026 calling for legal reforms alongside disarmament, yet no legislation has followed. Reuters warns that failure could reignite one of the Middle East's longest conflicts, which has killed over 40,000 people since 1984.
sections.whyItMatters: The Iran war has removed whatever urgency Ankara felt to move quickly on Kurdish reforms — and given the PKK a reason to keep its guard up. A frozen process isn't a failed one, but the longer it stays stuck, the harder it becomes to restart.

sections.heading: Erdogan Deepens Ties with Kazakhstan and Backs Iraq's New Government
sections.body:
President Erdogan visited Astana for the Türkiye-Kazakhstan Business Forum, signing 13 bilateral documents and a friendship and strategic partnership declaration targeting €13 billion in bilateral trade (currently approaching $10 billion). Kazakhstan has become Turkey's top trade partner in the Turkic world in 2025, with Kazakh GDP growing 6.5% and per capita income approaching $15,000. Around 5,500 Turkish companies have invested $6 billion in Kazakhstan, completing over 500 projects worth $30 billion. Both sides committed to revitalizing the Trans-Caspian Middle Corridor through integrated railway, port, and digital customs systems.

Separately, Turkey welcomed Iraq's new government under Prime Minister Ali al-Zaidi, who was sworn in May 14 with a partial cabinet after months of deadlock. Turkey's foreign ministry pledged continued support for Iraq's stability and regional integration.
sections.whyItMatters: With Hormuz disruptions threatening sea lanes, the Middle Corridor push is commercially sensible — but also positions Turkey as the gatekeeper of a cargo route that bypasses both Russia and Iran. Backing Zaidi gives Ankara a friendly voice in Baghdad as Iranian influence in Iraq evolves post-war.

sections.heading: Turkey-Armenia Direct Trade Restored After 33 Years
sections.body:
Turkey lifted customs restrictions on Armenia on May 13, enabling direct trade classification for the first time since 1993. Previously, goods between the two countries had to transit through third nations — primarily Georgia — with Armenian or Turkish destinations erased from customs paperwork. Armenia's State Revenue Committee confirmed that as of May 11, businesses can now import from Turkey directly in their own name without intermediaries.

The change is part of the confidence-building measures within the Armenia-Turkey normalization process underway since 2022. Armenia welcomed the move, calling it an important step toward normalizing relations and reopening the border. However, the land border itself remains closed — trade still physically transits Georgia, but the removal of bureaucratic barriers is expected to reduce costs and shorten delivery times.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the most tangible economic breakthrough in Turkish-Armenian relations in over three decades — but it stops well short of border reopening. With Azerbaijan's Karabakh victory secured and US engagement in the South Caucasus intensifying, the calculus has shifted enough for incremental progress, though full normalization remains distant.

sections.heading: Washington Panel Previews Ankara Summit as Belgian Minister Praises Turkish Defense
sections.body:
The Turkish Presidency's Directorate of Communications held a high-level panel in Washington titled 'The Turkish-American Alliance at the Heart of NATO's New Geopolitics', the fifth stop in a global dialogue series ahead of the July Ankara Summit. Former US Ambassador James Jeffrey stated that Turkey's contribution to NATO security is 'on par with that of the US' in several theatres. Ambassador Sedat Önal stressed that NATO's relevance depends on alliance unity and adaptation to evolving conditions.

Meanwhile, Belgian Defense Minister Theo Francken called for Turkey's inclusion in the EU's SAFE (Security Action for Europe) Program, describing it as Belgium's official policy. 'Türkiye is a role model in defense — lots of innovation, UAVs, laser technologies,' Francken said, announcing Belgium's largest-ever economic delegation to Turkey (500–600 entrepreneurs) for June 2026. Belgium will also appoint a defense attaché focused on Turkish weapon systems.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey's defense industry is generating diplomatic capital beyond the usual NATO framework. A senior EU minister publicly advocating for Turkish inclusion in a European defense fund — and calling Turkey a 'role model' — would have been unthinkable five years ago. The question is whether that warmth survives the July summit's political pressures.

bottomLine: The Iran war is reshaping Turkey's economic and strategic calculations in real time. The CBRT's 26% inflation forecast and a lira held together by carry-trade inflows and tight policy expose the fragility of Ankara's macro stance. But Turkey is simultaneously leveraging the disruption — proposing a NATO fuel pipeline, pushing Middle Corridor logistics with Kazakhstan, restoring trade with Armenia, and positioning its defense industry as indispensable to European security. The PKK process is frozen, the Gaza flotilla is sailing again from Turkish shores, and the July NATO summit is weeks away. Turkey is playing every board at once, and the Iran war has made the stakes of each move higher.
sources: Türkiye Today, AGBI, Reuters, ILKHA, TRT World, Anadolu Agency, Jerusalem Post, Kurdpress, ArmRadio, Oil Price, Bloomberg, Daily Sabah, Telegraph, Turkish Minute, France24, Trading Economics, S&P Global
